execution_info.prompt_template:
Now you are an expert who is good at using commonsense for reasoning.
The following conversation noted between ’### ###’ involves several speakers.
###
{history}
###
Based on the above historical utterances, please use commonsense to infer the reaction of potential listeners in < "{speaker_id}" : "{utterance}" >, said with < {audio_features} >.
Output no more than 10 words:

inputs.history:
F: Oh, shut up.
M: We might get up a splendid little- a splendid little debate about this, you know, intemperate tots.
F: Not very funny, dear.  You'd better have some more brandy.
M: Very good idea.  I will
M: You better turn that off I think.
F: Why?
M: It's late and the people upstairs will get annoyed.
F: There aren't any people upstairs.  It's a photographer's studio.
M: There are people downstairs, I suppose
F: They're away in Tunis.
M: This is no time of year for Tunis.
F: Turn it on again, please.
M: I will do no such thing.
F: Very well, if you insist on being boorish and idiotic.
M: Turn it off.  It's driving me mad.
F: Go away.  Go away.  I hate you.
M: Turn it off.
F: You are far too temperamental.  Try to control yourself.
F: We swapped. Listen.
M: Listen--
F: Listen, indeed.  I am sick and tired of listening to you, you damn sadistic bully.
inputs.utterance: Listen, indeed.  I am sick and tired of listening to you, you damn sadistic bully.
inputs.audio_features: high speech rate, high pitch, and high intensity

dev.output: Startled, uncomfortable, and aware of extreme, escalating anger.

inputs.history:
M: There's nothing to it.  We come down here.  The grunions arrive.  They do their little fish business.  We go ooh, ah, look at the fish.  They go home.  We go home.
F: For heaven's sake, we are not going to see the grunions.
M: Why not?
F: We never do, do we?  Have we ever?
M: We've missed them twice.
M: That's not ever.
F: Twice is every time we've tried.  That's ever.
M: We'll see them this year.
F: No, we won't.  It's pointless.  It's like waiting up to see Santa Claus.  I feel as though my entire life is going to be spent standing on the beach with my eyes wide open and my hands clasped expectantly waiting for the fish to show up.  And the fish won't show up.
M: I thought you wanted to see them.
F: You don't have any idea what I'm saying.
M: No, of course, not.
F: I did want to see it.  I always do.  I keep thinking this time will be the time, but it never is.
M: It's just fish; nothing to get traumatized over.
F: For heaven's sake, this is not about fish.
M: I've been trying to work this backwards, but I can't seem to pinpoint the moment when we took a turn for the worse.
M: You were fine during the phone call from Marge.  Dinner was okay.  Doing the dishes was no problem.  Let's see, I took a shower.  Uh, you watched the news.  You said the grunions are running tonight.  I said great, let's go.  I feel lucky tonight.
M: Uh,okay.  So I'm putting on my jacket; putting out the animals;
M: uh, taking the car out of the gara- out of the garage.
F: Do you remember the first time we came to see it?
M: Uh.
inputs.utterance: Uh.
inputs.audio_features: low speech rate, medium pitch, and medium intensity

dev.output: Listeners perceive M as hesitant, struggling to recall, or realizing the implication.

inputs.history:
F: What happens if one of us dies?  Does the one that's left, still laugh?
M: Yes.  Yes, with all his might.
F: Well that's serious enough, isn't it?
M: No, no, it isn't.  Death's very laughable; such a cunning little mystery, all done with mirrors.
F: Darling, I believe you're talking nonsense.
M: So is everyone else in the long run.  Let's be superficial and pity the poor philosophers.  Let's blow trumpets and squeakers and- and enjoy the party as much as we can, like very small, quite idiotic little school children.
M: Let's savor the delight of the moment.  Come and kiss me, darling, before your body rots and worms pop in and out of your eye sockets.
F: Elliot, worms don't pop.
M: I don't mind what you do, see?  You can paint yourself bright green all over and dance naked in the Place Vendome and rush off madly with all the men in the world and I shan't say a word as long as you love me best.
F: Thank you, dear.  The same applies to you, except that if I catch you so much as looking at another woman, I will kill you.
M: Do you remember that awful scene we had in Venice?
F: Which particular one?
M: The one when you bought that little painted wooded snake and put it on my bed?
F: Oh, Charles; that was his name, Charles.  Oh, he did wriggle so beautifully.
M: Horrible thing.  I hated it.
F: I know you did.  You threw it out of the window into the Grand Canal.  I don't think I'll ever forgive you for that.
M: How long did that row last?
F: It went on intermittently for days.
M: The worst one was in Cannes when your curling irons burnt holes in my dressing gown.
F: It burnt my comb, too, and all the towels in the bathroom.
M: Line?
inputs.utterance: Line?
inputs.audio_features: low speech rate, low pitch, and low intensity

dev.output: Listeners realize it's a play, quiet surprise, await prompt.

inputs.history:
F: Not like that you're not.
M: I kissed you.
F: Like Larry's brother.  Do it like you, Chris.  What is it, Chris?
M: Let's drive somewhere.  I want to be alone with you.
F: No, what is it, Chris?  Your mother?
M: No, no, nothing like that.
F: Then what's wrong.  Even in your letters, there was something ashamed.
M: Yes, I suppose there has been.  But it's going from me.
F: You've got to tell me.
M: I don't know where to start.
F: It wouldn't work this way.
M: It's all mixed up with so many other things.  You remember overseas, I was in command of a company?
F: Yeah, sure.
M: Well I lost them.
F: How many?
M: Just about all.
F: Oh, gees.
M: It takes a little time to toss that off because they weren't just men.
M: For instance, one time it'd been raining several days.  And this kid came to me and gave me his last pair of dry socks; put them in my pocket.  That's only a little thing, but that's the kind of guys I had.
M: They didn't die.  They killed themselves for each other.  I mean that exactly.  A little more selfish and they'd have been here today.
M: And I got this idea watching them go down, everything was being destroyed, see?  But, to me, it seemed there was one new thing that was made,
inputs.utterance: And I got this idea watching them go down, everything was being destroyed, see?  But, to me, it seemed there was one new thing that was made,
inputs.audio_features: medium speech rate, low pitch, and medium intensity

dev.output: Listeners are somber, attentive, and curious about the 'new thing'.

inputs.history:
F: Yes we did. Do you want to keep going?
M: Yeah, let's keep going.
F: Just give it some more thought.
M: I've given it three years' of thought.  I'd hope that if
M: father forgot about Larry, we'd have a regular wedding and everything happy.  If I can't get that here, then I'll have to get out.
F: What the hell is this?
M: I'll get out.  I'll get married and live someplace else.  Maybe New York.
F: Are you crazy?
M: I've been a good son too long; a good sucker.  I'm through with it.
F: You've got a business here.  What the hell is this?
M: The business?  The business doesn't inspire me.
F: Must you be inspired?
M: Yes.  I like getting an hour a day.  If I have to grub for money all day long, at least at evening, I want it beautiful.
M: I want a family.  I want some kids.  I want to build something I can give myself to.  Annie's in the middle of that.  Now where do I find it?
F: You mean -- tell me something.  You mean you'd really leave the business?
M: Yes, on this I would.
F: You don't want to think like that.
M: Then you help me stay here.
F: Alright.  But just don't think like that because what the hell did we work for?  That's all for you, Chris.  The whole shooting match is just for you.
M: I know that.  Just you help me stay here.
F: But don't think that way, you hear me?
inputs.utterance: But don't think that way, you hear me?
inputs.audio_features: medium speech rate, medium pitch, and medium intensity

dev.output: M feels unheard and frustrated, increasing the tension.

inputs.history:
F: You've got to tell me.
M: I don't know where to start.
F: It wouldn't work this way.
M: It's all mixed up with so many other things.  You remember overseas, I was in command of a company?
F: Yeah, sure.
M: Well I lost them.
F: How many?
M: Just about all.
F: Oh, gees.
M: It takes a little time to toss that off because they weren't just men.
M: For instance, one time it'd been raining several days.  And this kid came to me and gave me his last pair of dry socks; put them in my pocket.  That's only a little thing, but that's the kind of guys I had.
M: They didn't die.  They killed themselves for each other.  I mean that exactly.  A little more selfish and they'd have been here today.
M: And I got this idea watching them go down, everything was being destroyed, see?  But, to me, it seemed there was one new thing that was made,
M: a kind of responsibility.  Man for man.  You understand me?  And then I went home and it was incredible.
M: There was no meaning in it here.  The whole thing to them was some sort of bus accident.  Then I went to work for dad and that whole rat race again.  I felt, what you said, ashamed somehow
M: because nobody was changed.  It seemed to make suckers out of a lot of guys.  I felt wrong to be alive;
M: to open that bank book; to drive the new car; to see the new refrigerator.  I mean, you can take these things out of a war, but when you drive the new car, you have to know that it came out of the love a man can have for a man.
M: You have to be a little better because of that.  Otherwise, we have is really loot and there's blood on it.  I don't want to take any of it.  I guess that included you.
F: And do you still feel that way?
M: I want you now, Annie.
F: Because you mustn't feel that way anymore, Chris.  Because you have a right to everything you have, everything, Chris; to me, too.
inputs.utterance: Because you mustn't feel that way anymore, Chris.  Because you have a right to everything you have, everything, Chris; to me, too.

M: Just about all.
F: Oh, gees.
M: It takes a little time to toss that off because they weren't just men.
M: For instance, one time it'd been raining several days.  And this kid came to me and gave me his last pair of dry socks; put them in my pocket.  That's only a little thing, but that's the kind of guys I had.
M: They didn't die.  They killed themselves for each other.  I mean that exactly.  A little more selfish and they'd have been here today.
M: And I got this idea watching them go down, everything was being destroyed, see?  But, to me, it seemed there was one new thing that was made,
M: a kind of responsibility.  Man for man.  You understand me?  And then I went home and it was incredible.
M: There was no meaning in it here.  The whole thing to them was some sort of bus accident.  Then I went to work for dad and that whole rat race again.  I felt, what you said, ashamed somehow
M: because nobody was changed.  It seemed to make suckers out of a lot of guys.  I felt wrong to be alive;
M: to open that bank book; to drive the new car; to see the new refrigerator.  I mean, you can take these things out of a war, but when you drive the new car, you have to know that it came out of the love a man can have for a man.
M: You have to be a little better because of that.  Otherwise, we have is really loot and there's blood on it.  I don't want to take any of it.  I guess that included you.

F: I'm just not.
M: For real?
F: Yes.
M: I don't get it.  First time we came here to see it, you said it was the best night of your life.  And last year, I remember distinctly you were so excited to get here, you stubbed your toe rushing me out the door and didn't even notice until we were in the car.
F: Last year, you will also remember, our shoes got wet.  I got sand in my panties.  We got into a big fight over nothing.  You got a sore throat and we did not see the grunions.
M: We weren't in a good spot.
F: We didn't see them the first time either.
M: We just weren't in a good spot.  This is a much better spot.  I've got good instincts for these things.  Look there.  What's that?  Is that -
M: no, it's just seaweed, isn't it?  Oh, man, this is great.  I wouldn't miss this for anything.  I mean, think about this.  I know you're not interested, but just think about this for a minute.
M: Out there someplace is this huge mass of silver fish heading this way.  They don't know why.  We don't know why.  It wasn't a decision.  They didn't take a vote.  No one sent them an invitation or a map;
M: just some little instinctual time release firecracker went off in their little bodies and all as one, they turned and formed a line as long as the California coast and started swimming.
M: They're swimming right now as if that's the only thing that matters.  It's single mindedness with no mind.  Urgency that's pure urge.
M: They don't know what drives them.  They don't give it a name or ask a question.  They just turn, as one, and start the swim that brings them here; to this place, where we stand, for a massive celebration of sex and death.  Oh, man, I'm giving myself goose bumps.  All my hairs are popping up.
F: That's the wind.
M: Well so what did you think?
F: About what?

M: It all depends on how well we've played.
F: What happens if one of us dies?  Does the one that's left, still laugh?
M: Yes.  Yes, with all his might.
F: Well that's serious enough, isn't it?
M: No, no, it isn't.  Death's very laughable; such a cunning little mystery, all done with mirrors.
F: Darling, I believe you're talking nonsense.
M: So is everyone else in the long run.  Let's be superficial and pity the poor philosophers.  Let's blow trumpets and squeakers and- and enjoy the party as much as we can, like very small, quite idiotic little school children.
M: Let's savor the delight of the moment.  Come and kiss me, darling, before your body rots and worms pop in and out of your eye sockets.
F: Elliot, worms don't pop.
M: I don't mind what you do, see?  You can paint yourself bright green all over and dance naked in the Place Vendome and rush off madly with all the men in the world and I shan't say a word as long as you love me best.
F: Thank you, dear.  The same applies to you, except that if I catch you so much as looking at another woman, I will kill you.
M: Do you remember that awful scene we had in Venice?
F: Which particular one?
M: The one when you bought that little painted wooded snake and put it on my bed?
F: Oh, Charles; that was his name, Charles.  Oh, he did wriggle so beautifully.
M: Horrible thing.  I hated it.
F: I know you did.  You threw it out of the window into the Grand Canal.  I don't think I'll ever forgive you for that.
M: How long did that row last?
F: It went on intermittently for days.
M: The worst one was in Cannes when your curling irons burnt holes in my dressing gown.
F: It burnt my comb, too, and all the towels in the bathroom.
inputs.utterance: It burnt my comb, too, and all the towels in the bathroom.
inputs.audio_features: high speech rate, medium pitch, and high intensity

dev.output: Listeners would likely be amused by the dramatic recounting of chaos.
